name: Liquid Chromatography Raman Spectroscopy
description:
### Context

This data set was produced by Hirotsugu Hiramatsu as part of his experiment revolving around the enhancement of Raman signal utilizing a vertical flow method.  

### Content

The raman_mix files contain raman spectral data for the mixtures containing 4 amino acids (glycine, leucine, phenylalanine, tryptophan). 
Uv_spectrum contains spectral data utilizing UV.
AminoAcids file is comprised several sheets. Each sheet corresponds to the data of a single amino acid and is labeled accordingly. 

### Acknowledgements

 The spectral data was produced by Professor Hirotsugu Hiramatsu (hiramatu@nctu.edu.tw) and processed by Professor Stefano Rini (stefano@nctu.edu.tw).

### Inspiration

We seek to change the method of analysis of the spectral data. The conventional methods usually revolve around the use of supervised methods such as PCA and require an expert on the field to correctly reconstruct analyte spectra and elution patterns. Our proposed method utilizes unsupervised learning methods to minimize the requirement of expert and input.
citeAs: @misc{Rini_2020, title={An efficient label-free analyte detection algorithm for time-resolved spectroscopy}, author={Rini, Stefano and Hiramatsu, Hirotsugu}, year={2020}, eprint={2011.07470}, archivePrefix={arXiv}, primaryClass={eess.SP}}
rai:dataCollection: Liquid chromatography coupled Raman spectroscopy (LC-Raman) with vertical flow method. Mobile Raman probe attached to HPLC system (hydrophobic resin column, H₂O to acetonitrile gradient, 7 mL/min flow rate, 50 µL injection). Time-resolved acquisition at 0.2 s exposure per spectrum frame (~100 frames per 20 s window per elution peak).
rai:dataPreprocessingProtocol: Fluorescence background removed by asymmetric polynomial fitting at each time step. Solvent spectrum (H₂O/ACE) removed by linear regression. Savitzky-Golay filter applied for smoothing. Resulting time-frequency matrix used directly for unsupervised algorithm benchmarking.
rai:dataAnnotationProtocol: Time-resolved LC-Raman elution data for leucine (100 mM injection concentration). Labels are elution-time concentration profiles. Each amino acid eluted separately through an HPLC column (hydrophobic resin) using an H₂O-to-acetonitrile gradient mobile phase (7 mL/min, 50 µL injection). Data collected at NCTU, Taiwan (Lo, Hiramatsu lab). Purpose is benchmarking unsupervised label-free analyte detection algorithms; no per-spectrum expert labels required.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: No personal or sensitive information is included. All spectra originate from pure amino acid aqueous solutions without any human identifiers.
rai:dataBiases: Single-laboratory, single-instrument benchmark dataset. Each amino acid measured in isolation — simultaneous multi-analyte elution not covered. Analyte concentrations are in the millimolar range (100 mM for glycine and leucine, 55 mM for phenylalanine and tryptophan); performance of detection algorithms at lower physiological concentrations is unknown. arXiv preprint — findings not yet peer-reviewed at time of dataset release.
rai:dataLimitations: Small benchmark dataset covering only four amino acids in single-analyte conditions. The detection challenge arises from millimolar analyte concentrations against a strong H₂O/ACE solvent background. No explicit license stated; CC BY 4.0 assumed — verify with original authors.
rai:dataSocialImpact: LC-Raman with vertical flow enables real-time, label-free detection of analytes in chromatographic separations without destructive sampling. Applications include pharmaceutical process monitoring, metabolomics, and inline quality control in biomanufacturing.
rai:dataUseCases: Benchmarking time-resolved analyte detection algorithms, unsupervised elution peak detection, PCA/NMF/MCR-ALS dimensionality reduction, LC-Raman method development.
prov:wasGeneratedBy: Data collection: Laboratory measurements on chemical samples including pure compounds, mixtures, and solutions. Preprocessing: Spectra may have undergone baseline correction, smoothing, normalisation, and wavenumber calibration. Exact steps are described in the accompanying publication. Annotation: Ground-truth target values measured by validated reference analytical methods (e.g., enzymatic assays, dynamic light scattering, gas chromatography, or DFT computation).
